input_excerpt:
James Carter

Jacksonville, NC · (910) 555-0148 · jcarter88@email.com

SUMMARY

U.S. Marine Corps veteran with six years of experience leading teams, managing equipment, and getting results under pressure. Known for reliability, discipline, and strong leadership. Looking to bring these skills to a civilian team in operations, logistics, or a related field.

EXPERIENCE
Team Leader / Sergeant (E-5),

findings.title: Unverifiable trait claims in summary
findings.quote: Known for reliability, discipline, and strong leadership.
findings.why: "Known for" asks the civilian reader to take your word for it. A hiring reader in operations or logistics treats unverifiable trait assertions as filler and skips them, so this sentence spends prime summary space proving nothing about your fit.
findings.task: Cut this sentence or replace it with one specific, verifiable outcome your leadership produced. Keep traits only if a bullet on the page demonstrates them.

findings.title: Clearance status may be stale
findings.why: Your record shows separation in 2025. Clearances lapse without a sponsoring position, and a recruiter who checks will read an inaccurate "Active" as carelessness or inflation on the one line where precision matters most.
findings.task: Confirm and state only the status true today, with its date (for example, eligibility within the reinstatement window). Which formulation is accurate is a fact only you can verify.

findings.title: Vague scope of leadership and management
findings.quote: Supervised and trained a team of up to 12 people, managing their performance, safety, and daily tasks.
findings.why: "Up to 12" signals a variable or peak number rather than a consistent span of control, and a civilian operations hiring reader cannot tell your typical authority. "Managing their performance" could mean formal reviews or informal task direction — the reader can't gauge the responsibility level.
findings.task: Decide the typical team size you consistently led and clarify what "managing performance" concretely meant (formal evaluations, scheduling, or task assignment). Draft the revision yourself using only what you can defend.

findings.title: Equipment accountability lacks operating context
findings.quote: Maintained 100% accountability of equipment valued at over $500,000 with no losses.
findings.why: Military property accountability is real, but a civilian logistics reader needs to know what "accountability" involved — inventory tracking, custody signatures, audits, or physical security. Without that, the dollar figure reads as prestige rather than a transferable process skill.
findings.task: Name the accountability activity (for example, the inventory or audit process you ran) and how often, so the reader sees the transferable logistics function behind the number.

findings.title: Operations bullet is generic and unquantified
findings.quote: Planned and carried out operations and training, consistently meeting deadlines and standards.
findings.why: "Operations and training" and "standards" are undefined for a civilian reader, and "consistently meeting deadlines" states an expectation, not an achievement. This bullet could describe almost any job and gives an operations employer nothing to distinguish you.
findings.task: Choose one specific planning task with an observable result (a measurable outcome, timeline, or scope) and describe it in civilian terms. Only use facts you can verify.

findings.title: Leadership training not translated for civilian reader
findings.quote: Leadership Training: Completed Marine Corps leadership courses (supervisor-level).
findings.why: A civilian hiring reader cannot judge what "supervisor-level" Marine Corps courses covered or their length. Named or described, this could show real management preparation; as written it is opaque and easy to skip.
findings.task: Confirm the actual course name(s) and duration, and note the skills covered in plain language. Do not overstate equivalence to any civilian certification.

findings.title: Recognition claim without specifics
findings.quote: earned recognition for leadership and dependability
findings.why: This vague reference to "recognition" duplicates the awards you already list and adds an unverifiable trait claim. A civilian reader gets no new information and may read it as padding.
findings.task: Either tie this to a specific award or event you can name, or cut it to avoid repeating the trait language flagged in your summary.

note: The draft shows genuine leadership experience but leans on unverifiable traits, a possibly stale clearance, and untranslated military context that a civilian operations or logistics reader cannot evaluate.
